text: Hello everyone. This is a video of a patient who underwent a robotic ETEP incisional hernia repair. This case is interesting because I approached this operation only knowing that the patient had two prior robotic hernia repairs, but did not know the technique that was utilized to fix the two previous hernias. He is a sixty-one year old male, five foot six, two hundred twelve pounds, with a BMI of thirty-three kilograms per meter squared. His past medical history is significant for well-controlled type two diabetes, obstructive sleep apnea, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, mild renal insufficiency with a creatinine of one point two, BPH, lower extremity edema. He is a non-smoker.

His past surgical history is significant for a robotic umbilical hernia repair with mesh, but the mesh and technique are unknown. About a year later, he noticed a recurrence. Three years later, he underwent a robotic incisional hernia repair. Again, the mesh and the technique are unknown. He has undergone a CT of the abdomen and pelvis which I have seen. Unfortunately, the CT is not available for this video. On the CT, the tissue between the rectus muscle appears more like diastasis rather than a hernia. However, on physical exam, he has a six centimeter area of weakness within a six centimeter diastasis. This area is above the umbilicus and according to the patient, it's getting weaker and weaker and he feels that there is an effect on his core strength because of this weakness.

The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon clears the ipsilateral retro-rectus space to identify the linea alba. The view shifts towards the pelvis and arcuate line, with the retroactive space mostly cleared. The surgeon uses scissors to start the crossover, entering the preperitoneal space through an incision in the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon navigates back to a challenging area, encountering sticky fat with embedded old mesh and V-lock sutures. Dissection proceeds between the posterior rectus sheath and the hernia sac, aiming for a bloodless field using cautery. The surgeon enters the peritoneal cavity with scissors near the arcuate line, below the hernia site.

The surgeon returns to the hernia area, attempting to dissect the hernia sac while navigating between visible blue permanent V-lock sutures and underlying tissue. The blue sutures appear to have been used to close the anterior fascia or diastasis, while white absorbable V-lock sutures are seen securing the mesh circumferentially. The surgeon cautiously uses cautery, avoiding excessive energy near the peritoneum, which is visibly thin. Foreign material, including mesh, complicates the dissection, requiring careful navigation between the mesh and sutures. The peritoneum is inspected for bowel attachment, ensuring safe cautery application.

The surgeon uses cautery to dissect around the blue Pro Grip mesh, which is visible with its blue markings in the prepartineal space. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid injury to the anterior rectus sheath on the patient's right side. The surgeon presses down with the left hand to maintain the correct plane along the mesh, ensuring adequate mesh overlap caudal to the umbilicus.

The surgeon cuts the left transverse salus fascia, revealing preperitoneal fat, and proceeds to cut the right transverse salus fascia to access the right retrorectus space. The left hand guides the dissection towards the linea alba, facilitating the identification of the contralateral retro-rectal space from the arcuate line. The surgeon carefully dissects in the preperitoneal space, aiming to open the posterior rectus sheath without damaging the junction with the peritoneum. Visible mesh and V-lock sutures are peeled off, and the dissection continues behind the posterior rectus sheath, maintaining the integrity of the peritoneum. The surgeon uses the left hand to guide the opening of the posterior rectus sheath, preparing to re-approximate the six-centimeter diastasis up to the xiphoid.

The surgeon performs contralateral retrorectus dissection near the arcuate line, cutting through the areolar avascular tissue between the transversalis fascia and retrorectus fat. The diastasis narrows significantly by the xiphoid. A large piece of mesh is visible on the patient's right side, with gaps in the posterior layer being closed using V-Lock sutures. Old mesh is removed and temporarily placed in the right lower quadrant. The surgeon closes small posterior defects with figure-of-eight sutures and addresses a larger defect near the old mesh with a Threo V-Lock suture.

The surgeon re-approximates the linea alba, bringing the medial edges of the rectus muscles together by grabbing both the anterior and posterior rectus sheaths. Progressive tensioning is applied to manage the slack of the long suture. A second suture is started, and bites are taken from the diastasis to reduce seroma size. Pneumoperitoneum is lowered to facilitate closure. The diastasis is repaired, stopping four centimeters below the xiphoid, allowing for mesh overlap. A 20 cm by 34 cm mesh is inserted through an 8 mm port and unfurled to cover the reconstructed linea alba.
